drafts.title: Dropout in Neural Networks
drafts.body:
Dropout is a technique applied during neural network training in which units (neurons) or their connections are randomly omitted at each training step. Originally introduced as a practical regularization heuristic, dropout reduces overfitting by preventing complex co-adaptations among neurons. In the standard regularization interpretation, dropout is active during training but switched off at test time, so the full network is used to produce point predictions.

A deeper theoretical understanding reveals that a neural network trained with dropout can be interpreted as performing approximate Bayesian inference. Under this view, each forward pass with dropout active corresponds to drawing a sample from an approximate posterior distribution over the network's weights. This connection means that the same mathematical mechanism admits two complementary descriptions: one rooted in regularization theory and one in Bayesian probability.

The distinction between these two framings has concrete consequences at inference time. The regularization perspective prescribes disabling dropout after training to obtain deterministic predictions. The Bayesian perspective, by contrast, prescribes keeping dropout active during inference — a procedure known as Monte Carlo (MC) dropout — and performing multiple stochastic forward passes. The variability across these passes provides an estimate of predictive uncertainty, something the standard regularization usage does not supply. Thus, while the training procedure is identical under both interpretations, the inference procedures diverge: one yields a single point estimate, the other yields a distribution that quantifies model uncertainty. The Bayesian framing therefore adds substantive capability — principled uncertainty estimation — that the regularization framing alone does not provide.

drafts.title: Bayesian Inference in Deep Learning
drafts.body:
Bayesian inference applied to deep learning seeks to maintain and reason about a full posterior distribution over a neural network's weights, rather than settling on a single point estimate. In principle this enables principled uncertainty quantification, better calibrated predictions, and more robust decision-making. In practice, exact Bayesian inference over the high-dimensional weight spaces of modern neural networks is intractable, motivating a range of approximate methods.

One notable approximate Bayesian method arises from the reinterpretation of dropout. A neural network with dropout applied at both training and test time can be understood as performing variational inference, where each stochastic forward pass samples from an approximate posterior. This technique, known as Monte Carlo (MC) dropout, provides a computationally inexpensive route to approximate Bayesian inference because it requires no modification to the standard training procedure — only that dropout remain active during prediction. By aggregating the outputs of many stochastic forward passes, one obtains both a mean prediction and a measure of predictive uncertainty.

This example illustrates a broader theme: practical training heuristics originally motivated by non-Bayesian reasoning can sometimes be shown, after the fact, to correspond to well-defined Bayesian approximation schemes. Such correspondences enrich the theoretical understanding of widely used techniques and unlock additional functionality — most importantly, uncertainty estimation — that the original non-Bayesian motivation did not anticipate. The two descriptions (regularization and Bayesian approximation) coexist as views of the same mathematical object, yet they are not interchangeable in practice because they prescribe different inference-time procedures and yield different outputs.
